paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 13: The Spelling Bee (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 girl who represents her school at the National Inter-School Spelling Bee finals and loses on a word she actually knew, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [10 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format, pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 7 short-answer items (Q1–Q7) and a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q8) are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The National Inter-School Spelling Bee finals were held at a hall in Bishan on a Saturday morning. I had taken the MRT there with my form teacher, Mr Tan. I was in Sec 2 and I was the only finalist from my school. I had been preparing for six months.

Paragraph 2
The first five rounds were easy. I spelled 'cinnamon', 'questionnaire', 'kaleidoscope', 'aquarium' and 'rendezvous'. After each correct answer the judge nodded, and I walked back to my seat trying not to smile too widely. I could see my parents in the third row.

Paragraph 3
In the sixth round the judge called my name and read out my word: 'fastidious'. I knew it. I had drilled it the night before. It meant being very careful about details — Mr Tan had taught me that. My heart jumped, but in a happy way. I opened my mouth to spell it.

Paragraph 4
Then I made a mistake. I did not wait for the judge to finish her usual second reading. I did not ask for the word in a sentence. I rushed. 'F-A-S-T-I-D-E-O-U-S,' I said quickly. The hall went quiet. 'I'm sorry,' the judge said gently. 'That is incorrect.' My ears began to ring.

Paragraph 5
I walked off the stage and sat down beside Mr Tan. He did not say anything at first. He passed me a small packet of tissues. I did not cry, but I held the tissues anyway. The competition continued on the stage. I heard the words being called but I could not really hear them.

Paragraph 6
On the bus home, I finally spoke. 'I knew that word,' I said. 'I really knew it.' Mr Tan nodded once. 'I should have slowed down,' I said. 'I should have asked for the sentence. I should have waited for the second reading.' He still did not say anything. The bus turned past the kopitiam at the end of my road.

Paragraph 7
'The hardest losses, Hui Lin,' Mr Tan said at last, 'are the ones you could have won.' He looked at me kindly. 'Now you know why your O-Levels will tell you the same thing one day. Knowing is not enough. You must also slow down. You must also check.' I stared at the back of the seat in front of me. I did not say anything. But I made myself remember every word.

Paragraph 8
That night, at my desk at home in Yishun, I wrote the word 'fastidious' on a small piece of paper. I spelled it correctly. I taped it to the edge of my study lamp where I would see it every evening. It is still there today.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past examination paper. MRT: Singapore's Mass Rapid Transit train system; kopitiam: a traditional coffee shop; O-Levels: the Singapore-Cambridge GCE Ordinary Level examinations.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what details show that the narrator had taken the spelling bee seriously? [1]
questions.answer: she had been preparing for six months and she was the only finalist from her school

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, what does the detail that the narrator 'tried not to smile too widely' suggest about her? [1]
questions.answer: she was pleased and confident with her performance, but she was trying not to show off / she was holding back her happiness so as not to look proud in front of the other competitors

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 4, explain in your own words why the narrator made a mistake on 'fastidious' even though she knew the word. [1]
questions.answer: she was too eager and rushed her answer without waiting for the second reading or asking for the word in a sentence, so she spelled it carelessly

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the phrase 'my ears began to ring' (Paragraph 4)? [1]
questions.answer: it shows that the shock and disappointment of being told she was wrong overwhelmed her physically — she could no longer hear the hall clearly because the moment felt so loud and stunning to her

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 5, why does Mr Tan hand the narrator a packet of tissues even though she does not cry? [1]
questions.answer: he wants to comfort her quietly without forcing her to say anything — the tissues are a wordless act of kindness that lets her hold something while she copes with her disappointment

questions.stem: Q6. Explain what Mr Tan means in Paragraph 7 when he says, 'The hardest losses are the ones you could have won.' [1]
questions.answer: he means that losing because of your own avoidable mistake hurts more than losing because the task was too hard — it is harder to accept failure when you know you had the ability to succeed

questions.stem: Q7. What does the narrator's action of taping the word 'fastidious' to her study lamp (Paragraph 8) suggest? [1]
questions.answer: she wants to be reminded every day of the lesson she learnt — that knowing a thing is not enough, she must slow down and check / she is determined never to make the same mistake again

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the morning of the spelling bee unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: confident / confident-still / devastated / wiser / bored / suspicious / angry / proud. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Spelling Bee' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the day unfolds — from confident as she finishes the first five rounds easily, to confident-still in the sixth round when she hears 'fastidious' and recognises a word she had drilled the night before, to devastated when she rushes the spelling and is told she is incorrect, ending wiser that night as she writes the word out correctly and tapes it to her study lamp. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2 as she finishes the first five rounds, spelling words like 'cinnamon' and 'rendezvous' correctly while her mother watches from the third row? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 3 when the judge reads out the word 'fastidious' and her heart jumps because she had drilled exactly that word the night before? [1]
Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4 after she rushes the answer, the judge says 'I'm sorry, that is incorrect,' and her ears begin to ring? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8 that night at her desk in Yishun, writing 'fastidious' correctly on a small piece of paper and taping it to her study lamp where she will see it every evening? [1]
